title: Memoization
description: Finally fixing naive Fibonacci: caching each subproblem's answer the first time it's computed, turning O(2ⁿ) into O(n).
objectives: Implement memoized Fibonacci using a cache
Understand exactly how caching eliminates redundant recursive calls
See the complexity improvement from O(2ⁿ) to O(n) concretely
Recognize the general memoization pattern: check cache, compute if missing, store, return

sections.text: 1. Recap: Time to Actually Fix Fibonacci
Recursion in DSA showed naive Fibonacci recomputing fibonacci(2) multiple times, independently, giving O(2ⁿ) time. Dynamic Programming named this: overlapping subproblems. Memoization is the fix.

Memoization caches the result of each subproblem the first time it's computed, so later calls with the same input return the cached answer instantly instead of recomputing it — a top-down DP approach, keeping the natural recursive structure.

sections.title: What changed from naive Fibonacci
points: Exactly two new lines: check cache.count(n) before computing, and cache[n] = result after computing
The first time fibonacci(2) is needed, it's genuinely computed and cached
Every SUBSEQUENT time fibonacci(2) is needed (from any other branch of the recursion), it's returned instantly from cache — never recomputed
fibonacci(40) — which would take an astronomical number of calls naively — returns almost instantly with memoization

sections.text: There are only n distinct subproblems (fibonacci(0) through fibonacci(n)) — and with caching, each one is genuinely computed exactly once. Every other call is an O(1) cache lookup. Total work: O(n), a dramatic improvement matching the O(n) space traded for it (recall the time-space tradeoff from Space Complexity) — one cache entry per distinct subproblem.

This exact shape — check cache, compute if missing, store, return — applies to essentially any DP problem with overlapping subproblems, not just Fibonacci. Recognizing this template is often the key step in solving a new DP problem.

sections.title: Writing the answer down the first time, checking your notes every time after
sections.text: Recall Dynamic Programming's note-taking analogy, now made concrete: cache.count(n) is "do I already have a note for this?" and cache[n] = result is "write the note down." Every repeat question after the first gets answered by glancing at an existing note instead of solving it all over again.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to check the cache BEFORE doing the recursive work — the whole point is avoiding redundant computation, which only happens if the check comes first.
Forgetting to actually store the result in the cache after computing it — without that line, nothing is ever remembered, and it's back to naive recursion.
Using int instead of a wider type like long long for Fibonacci at larger n — the values grow quickly and can overflow (recall Type Modifiers).
Assuming memoization changes WHAT gets computed — it only avoids recomputing the SAME subproblem; the underlying recursive logic and base cases stay identical to the naive version.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is memoization?
questions.answer: Caching the result of each subproblem the first time it's computed, so later calls with the same input return the cached answer instantly instead of recomputing it.

questions.question: What two additions turn naive recursive Fibonacci into memoized Fibonacci?
questions.answer: Checking whether the answer is already in a cache before computing, and storing the computed result in that cache before returning it.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of memoized Fibonacci, and why is it so much better than the naive version?
questions.answer: O(n) — there are only n distinct subproblems, and memoization guarantees each is computed exactly once, with every repeat request answered by an O(1) cache lookup instead of full recomputation.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Memoization fixes naive Fibonacci's overlapping-subproblems inefficiency by caching each subproblem's answer the first time it's computed: check the cache before doing any work, and store the result in the cache before returning. Since fibonacci(n) only has n distinct subproblems, and memoization guarantees each is computed exactly once, the complexity drops from O(2ⁿ) to O(n) — every repeated request becomes an O(1) cache lookup instead of full recomputation. This check-cache/compute-if-missing/store/return pattern is the general template for memoizing essentially any problem with overlapping subproblems.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Memoization
sections.text: Memoization = write the answer down the first time, check your notes after that. Two new lines: check cache first, store result before returning. O(2ⁿ) → O(n).

sections.title: Climbing Stairs
sections.problemStatement: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Given n steps, return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.
hints: Apply this lesson's exact check-cache/compute-if-missing/store/return template to ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2).
A memo array (or unordered_map) sized n+1, initialized to a sentinel like -1, lets you distinguish 'not yet computed' from a legitimate answer.
The recursive structure is identical to naive Fibonacci — only the recurrence's exact numbers differ, proving memoization's template generalizes directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Naive recursion with no cache — this lesson's exact starting inefficiency, recomputing the same subproblems repeatedly.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Memoized recursion — check the cache first, compute and store only on a miss.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) — each of the n distinct subproblems computed exactly once
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the memo array plus recursion stack

sections.title: House Robber
sections.problemStatement: Given nums representing money in houses arranged in a line, return the maximum money robbable without robbing two directly adjacent houses.
hints: At house i, the choice is binary: rob it (and skip house i-1 entirely) or don't (carrying forward whatever the best was through i-1).
rob(i) = max(rob(i-1), rob(i-2) + nums[i]) — 'don't rob i' vs 'rob i, plus the best up through i-2'.
Apply the exact check-cache/compute-if-missing/store/return template from this lesson to this new recurrence.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Naive recursion with no cache — recomputes the same rob(i) subproblem repeatedly via different call paths.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Memoized recursion — check the cache first, compute and store only on a miss.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the memo array plus recursion stack
